entrance.JPG|thumb|right|{{Center|境内入口}}{{Small|鳥居は無く、代わりに{{jalink|注連縄|Q1887421}}が張られる。両脇には狛兎。}}]] '''調神社'''（つきじんじゃ{{Sfn|調神社（平凡社）|2004年}}）は、{{jalink|埼玉県|Q128186}}{{jalink|さいたま市|Q134635}}{{jalink|浦和区|Q1154583}}{{jalink|岸町 (さいたま市)|Q11476004|岸町}}三丁目にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|式内社}}で、{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|旧社格}}は{{jalink|県社}}。 別称は「調宮（つきのみや）」。社名の「ツキ」により月待信仰が古くからあり、{{jalink|狛犬|Q20155846}}ではなく狛ウサギがある神社として知られる。 === 祭神 === 祭神は次の3柱境内説明板。。 * {{jalink|天照大神|Q455602|天照大御神}}（あまてらすおおみかみ） * {{jalink|トヨウケビメ|Q2983724|豊宇気姫命}}（とようけびめのみこと） * {{jalink|スサノオ|Q272993|素盞嗚尊}}（すさのおのみこと） 祭神に関する伝説として、調神社に古くから伝わる七不思議（{{jalink|#七不思議||後述}}）のうちに「松が無いこと」がある{{Sfn|調神社（式内社）|1976年}}。その伝説では、当地に姉神・弟神がいたが弟神は大宮に行ってしまい、姉神が帰りを待っても弟神は帰って来なかったため、姉神はもう待つことを嫌ったという{{Sfn|調神社（式内社）|1976年}}。この伝説に見える姉神と弟神は、天照大神（当社祭神）と素盞嗚尊（大宮の{{jalink|氷川神社|Q703633}}祭神）にあたるといわれる{{Sfn|調神社（式内社）|1976年}}。 他に文献によれば、祭神を{{jalink|瀬織津姫|Q13579836|瀬織津比咩}}とする説{{efn|『{{jalink|日本総国風土記}}』には、当社の項目に「祭る所祭{{jalink|瀬織津姫|Q13579836|瀬織津比咩}}也」と記載されている。}}、調玉命とする説『武蔵国式内四十四座神社命附』、{{jalink|月読命}}とする説{{efn|『{{jalink|江戸名所図会|Q2704637}}』には、当社の項目に「祭神、{{jalink|月読命}}一坐、本地{{jalink|勢至菩薩|Q1465683}}」と記載されている{{sfn|江戸名所図会|1927|p=156}}。}}、日の神・{{jalink|ウガノミタマ||倉稲荷玉命}}の2柱とする説{{efn|『{{jalink|新編武蔵風土記稿|Q11503943}}』には、当社の項目に「当社は日の神、{{jalink|ウガノミタマ||倉稲荷玉命}}の二座を祭る所にして」と記載されている{{sfn|新編武蔵風土記稿 足立郡 岸村}}。}}、{{jalink|天照大神|Q455602}}・{{jalink|ウガノミタマ||宇賀御玉神}}の2柱とする説{{jalink|平田篤胤|Q2572749}} 著『調神社考証』・『{{jalink|特選神名牒|Q11571092}}』などがあった{{Sfn|調神社（式内社）|1976年}}。 === 歴史 === === 創建 === 社記（{{jalink|寛文|Q1146228}}8年（{{jalink|1668年|Q7595}}）の『調宮縁起』）によれば、第9代{{jalink|開化天皇|Q333548}}の{{jalink|乙酉|Q185265}}年3月に奉幣の社として創建されたという。また第10代{{jalink|崇神天皇|Q333530}}の時には{{jalink|伊勢神宮|Q687168}}斎主の{{jalink|倭姫命|Q4388732}}が参向し、清らかな岡である当地を選び、伊勢神宮に献上する調物（貢ぎ物・御調物）を納める倉を建て、武総野（{{jalink|武蔵国|Q907398|武蔵}}、{{jalink|上総国|Q1055394|上総}}・{{jalink|下総国|Q1053695|下総}}・{{jalink|安房国|Q1053044|安房}}、{{jalink|上野国|Q1054838|上野}}・{{jalink|下野国|Q1053699|下野}}）すなわち関東一円の初穂米・調の集積所と定めたとする。さらに{{jalink|宝亀|Q18520676}}2年（{{jalink|771年|Q29950}}）{{jalink|6月20日 (旧暦)|Q839899|6月20日}}には{{jalink|勅使|Q11399920}}として藤原朝臣常恣が奉幣を行ったので、これが例大祭（現在は新暦{{jalink|7月20日|Q2728}}）の起源になったと伝える{{Sfn|調神社（式内社）|1976年}}{{Sfn|調神社（神々）|1984年}}。 上記伝承の真偽は定かでないが、『式内社調査報告』等に於いても社名に「調」が見えることから、{{jalink|朝廷 (日本)|Q30253177|朝廷}}への調物（貢ぎ物・御調物）を納める御倉が調神社の前身になったと想定している{{Sfn|調神社（式内社）|1976年}}{{Sfn|調神社（神々）|1984年}}{{Sfn|調神社（平凡社）|2004年}}。関連伝承として調神社の七不思議のうちに「鳥居が無いこと」があり、その中で{{jalink|倭姫命|Q4388732}}の命により調物の運搬の妨げとなる鳥居・神門を取り払ったことによると伝えており、この伝承も調神社の前身が御倉であったことを表すものとされる{{Sfn|調神社（式内社）|1976年}}。そして伝承中で奉幣があったと伝える宝亀2年（771年）には{{jalink|武蔵国|Q907398}}の所属が{{jalink|東山道|Q1334011}}から{{jalink|東海道|Q1196306}}に変更されていることから『{{jalink|続日本紀|Q611399}}』宝亀2年（771年）10月{{jalink|己卯|Q1062557}}（27日）条。、この街道変更により調物が通らなくなって役割を終えた御倉が神聖視され、神社として奉斎されるようになったのが実際の創建になると推測されている{{Sfn|調神社（式内社）|1976年}}{{Sfn|調神社（神々）|1984年}}。 以上とは別に、『{{jalink|新撰姓氏録|Q1045362}}』や{{jalink|六国史|Q1151168|国史}}に見える調連・調首・調吉士・調忌寸一族（調氏）が奉斎したという説もある{{Sfn|調神社（式内社）|1976年}}。この調氏は渡来系氏族であるが、東国に{{jalink|渡来人|Q11561926}}の集団居住が多いこととの関連が指摘される{{Sfn|調神社（式内社）|1976年}}。当社の所在地名の「{{jalink|岸町 (さいたま市)|Q11476004|岸}}」は、「{{jalink|吉士|Q55526932}}」に由来するものとされる{{Sfn|岸村（平凡社）|2004年}}。そのほか、境内に多い槻の木（{{jalink|ケヤキ|Q920344}}）と「調」を関連付ける説{{jalink|国学|Q855159}}者の{{jalink|黒川春村|Q6536184}}は「社地一円に槻の古木にて頗喬木あり。是によりて思ふに調は槻の借字なり」と説を立てている。もある{{Sfn|調神社（神道大辞典）|1941年}}。 === 概史 === [[File:NDL-DC 1310959 Utagawa Kunisada 浦和調ノ社・油屋おこん crd.jpg|thumb|{{jalink|歌川国貞|Q467427}}木曽六十九驛『浦和 調ノ社・油屋おこん』（1852年・嘉永5年）]] {{jalink|延長 (元号)|Q1327274|延長}}5年（{{jalink|927年|Q30470}}）成立の『{{jalink|延喜式|Q1342448}}』{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932|神名帳}}では、{{jalink|武蔵国|Q907398}}{{jalink|足立郡|Q11637033}}に「調神社」と記載され、{{jalink|式内社}}に列している{{Sfn|調神社（式内社）|1976年}}。 {{jalink|中世|Q12554}}からは、「調」が「月」と同じ読みであることから、月待信仰と結びつくようになった{{Sfn|調神社（平凡社）|2004年}}。{{jalink|延元|Q1194159}}2年/{{jalink|建武 (日本)|Q1193970|建武}}4年（{{jalink|1337年|Q6039}}）には{{jalink|足利尊氏|Q297107}}の命で{{jalink|一色範行}}による社殿の復興があったが、{{jalink|天正|Q1145058}}18年（{{jalink|1590年|Q6764}}）には{{jalink|小田原征伐|Q2622890}}に伴う兵火で焼失したという{{Sfn|調神社（平凡社）|2004年}}。 {{jalink|江戸時代|Q184963}}、{{jalink|慶安|Q1146135}}2年（{{jalink|1649年|Q6945}}）には{{jalink|徳川家光|Q314481}}から{{jalink|朱印地}}7石が寄進された。この朱印状には「月読社」と記されているが{{Sfn|調神社（平凡社）|2004年}}、『調宮縁起』ではこの社名は誤りと記されている。しかし、その後の朱印状にもこの社名は継続して使われている。 {{jalink|別当寺|Q11396755}}は月山寺であり、{{jalink|玉蔵院 (さいたま市)|Q18336826|玉蔵院}}が兼帯した。月山寺には二十三夜堂が設けられ、{{jalink|月天|Q1061795|月天子}}の{{jalink|勢至菩薩|Q1465683}}が{{jalink|本尊|Q11520160}}としてまつられた{{Sfn|調神社（平凡社）|2004年}}。 『{{jalink|江戸名所図会|Q2704637}}』にも登載され、文人墨客もこの月待・月読の神を知ってか数多く参詣している。中でも{{jalink|寛政|Q1195131}}3年（{{jalink|1791年|Q7784}}）{{jalink|4月11日 (旧暦)|Q838941|4月11日}}には、俳人{{jalink|小林一茶|Q312709}}が深い森の中に鎮まる「月よみの宮」に額突いたことを『紀行』（一茶全集第5巻）に書いている。また、旅を好んだ僧の{{jalink|津田大浄}}は、{{jalink|文政|Q1327119}}2年（{{jalink|1819年|Q6967}}）に『{{jalink|遊歴雑記}}』に「二十三夜の宮と称す（中略）社の造営善尽し、美尽し、内のかざり心々の奉納の品々夥しく、辺鄙の駅路にくらぶれば目を驚かせり」と社頭の繁栄を記している{{Sfn|埼玉の神社（埼玉県神社庁）|1998年}}。また、{{jalink|文化 (元号)|Q1373146|文化}}4年（{{jalink|1807年|Q6909}}）から{{jalink|天保|Q1369045}}5年（{{jalink|1834年|Q7591}}）までの間に{{jalink|江戸|Q215646}}の郊外を旅した徳川{{jalink|清水家}}の御{{jalink|広敷用人|Q11485425}}の{{jalink|村尾正靖}}（号は嘉陵）は、記録の中で「{{jalink|勢至菩薩|Q1465683}}の森だという。本社、拝殿はみな銅葺き屋根で、建物全体を{{jalink|朱色|Q737438|朱}}に塗って、瑞籬をめぐらしている（中略）社の造りが仏教的でないので、往古には神を崇めていたものを、後になって仏に祀り変えたものであろう」と当時の様子を記している村尾嘉陵 著・阿部孝嗣 訳『江戸近郊道しるべ 現代語訳』{{jalink|講談社学術文庫|Q11632220}}、2013年。。 天保10年（{{jalink|1839年|Q7616}}）には、{{jalink|国学|Q855159}}者の{{jalink|平田篤胤|Q2572749}}が当社を参拝して『調神社考証』を著しているが、その著に於いて、当社は{{jalink|伊勢神宮|Q687168}}に納める{{jalink|初穂|Q11396544}}を入れる倉庫から発展した神社であるとした。 {{jalink|明治維新|Q8707}}後、{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}6年（{{jalink|1873年|Q7757}}）に{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534}}において{{jalink|郷社|Q134917316}}に列し、明治31年（{{jalink|1898年|Q7848}}）に{{jalink|県社}}に昇格した。 === 境内 === 社殿は本殿と拝殿が一体となった{{jalink|権現造|Q3155268}}で、{{jalink|安政|Q1327259}}5年（{{jalink|1858年|Q7684}}）の造営{{Sfn|調神社（平凡社）|2004年}}。それまで使用された旧本殿は、境内社の稲荷神社の社殿として現在も使用されている。 また境内の社叢はケヤキ・ムクノキの古木林を形成しており、「調神社の境内林」としてさいたま市指定天然記念物に指定されている。 File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) kagura-den.JPG|神楽殿 File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) shin-ike.JPG|神池の兎 File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) chozusha.JPG|手水舎の兎 File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) komausagi.JPG|狛兎 === 摂末社 === [[File:調神社 旧本殿（稲荷神社）.jpg|thumb|{{center|稲荷神社}}{{small|社殿は調神社旧本殿。平成29年に修理が完了。屋根を銅板葺きから柿葺きに戻した。}}
{{small|さいたま市指定文化財。}}]] * 稲荷神社 *: 社殿は調神社旧本殿。一間社{{jalink|流造|Q210684}}で、屋根は{{jalink|こけら葺||柿葺き}}（こけらぶき）。棟札によると{{jalink|享保|Q1145048}}18年（{{jalink|1733年|Q6972}}）の造営で、{{jalink|安政|Q1327259}}年間（{{jalink|1854年|Q7670}}-{{jalink|1860年|Q7691}}）まで本殿として使用された。調神社の月待信仰を反映して、多くの兎の彫物が施されている。この社殿はさいたま市指定文化財に指定されている。 * 調宮天神社 * 金毘羅神社 境内にはその他数社が鎮座する。 File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) keidaisha.JPG|{{small|調宮天神社（手前）と金毘羅神社（奥）}} [[ファイル:素戔嗚尊.jpg|代替文=素戔嗚尊|サムネイル|{{center|素戔嗚尊 石像}}]] === 祭事 === 調神社で年間に行われる主な祭事の一覧[http://www.stib.jp/info/data/tsuki.html 調神社]（公益社団法人さいたま観光国際協会「さいたま市の観光・国際交流情報」）。 。 * 歳旦祭 （{{jalink|1月1日|Q2150}}） * 節分祭 （{{jalink|2月3日|Q2315}}） * 春祭 （{{jalink|4月3日|Q2509}}） * 大祓式 （{{jalink|6月30日|Q2657}}） * 神幸祭 （{{jalink|7月19日|Q2726}}） * '''例祭''' （{{jalink|7月20日|Q2728}}） - 由緒で例大祭の起源とする旧暦6月20日の奉幣日を踏襲する。 * 秋祭 （{{jalink|11月23日|Q3021}}） * 大歳市祭（{{jalink|十二日まち|Q22123263}}）（{{jalink|12月12日|Q2369}}） === 文化財 === === さいたま市指定文化財 === * 有形文化財{{PDFlink|[https://www.city.saitama.lg.jp/004/005/006/003/p001267_d/fil/bunkazaiitiran.pdf 「さいたま市内指定・登録文化財一覧」]}}（さいたま市ホームページ）。 ** 旧本殿（付 木札 {{jalink|享保|Q1145048}}十八年の銘がある 一枚）（建造物） **: 江戸時代。{{jalink|昭和|Q327567}}53年（{{jalink|1978年|Q2483}}）{{jalink|3月29日|Q2459}}指定。 ** 扇面三十六歌仙絵 18面（絵画） **: 江戸時代。紙本墨書の{{jalink|三十六歌仙|Q671039|三十六歌仙絵}}で、36面のうち18面が現存する。{{jalink|寛文|Q1146228}}9年（{{jalink|1669年|Q7602}}）奉納。昭和53年（1978年）3月29日指定。 ** 神輿鳳凰（工芸品） **: 室町時代。{{jalink|神輿|Q979940}}の頂に取りつけられていた{{jalink|鳳凰|Q527525}}。昭和53年（1978年）3月29日指定。 ** 調神社扁額（書跡） **: 江戸時代。ケヤキ材の{{jalink|扁額|Q5358873}}。正面に楷書で「調神社」と記されている。{{jalink|享和|Q960763}}2年（{{jalink|1802年|Q6883}}）の{{jalink|松平定信|Q284038}}による筆。昭和53年（1978年）3月29日指定。 ** 調宮縁起（古文書） **: 江戸時代。寛文8年（{{jalink|1668年|Q7595}}）の{{jalink|玉蔵院 (さいたま市)|Q18336826|玉蔵院}}十二世寂堂による撰。昭和53年（1978年）3月29日指定。 * 天然記念物 ** 調神社の境内林 **: 昭和45年（{{jalink|1970年|Q2474}}）{{jalink|3月10日|Q2397}}指定。 === 七不思議 === 調神社では、次の七不思議が伝わる{{Sfn|調神社（式内社）|1976年}}{{Sfn|調神社（神々）|1984年}}。 # 鳥居が無いこと #: {{jalink|倭姫命|Q4388732}}の命で、調物の運搬の妨げとなる神門・鳥居を除いたことによるという。 # 松が無いこと #: 当地に姉神・弟神がいたが、そのうち弟神は大宮にいってしまい姉神が待っても帰ってこなかったため、姉神がもう待つことは嫌いだと言ったことに由来するという。また、姉神が待っているときに境内の松で目を突いたためともいう。 # 御手洗池（ひょうたん池とも、現在は消滅）の池に魚を放つと、その魚は片目になること # 兎が{{jalink|神使|Q10444973}}であること # {{jalink|日蓮|Q311478|日蓮聖人}}駒つなぎのケヤキ #: {{jalink|佐渡島|Q998623}}に流罪途中の日蓮が、当地で難産に苦しんでいた女性のためケヤキに駒を繋いで安産祈祷をしたことに由来するという。 # 蝿がいないこと # 蚊がいないこと === 現地情報 === ; 所在地 * {{jalink|埼玉県|Q128186}}{{jalink|さいたま市|Q134635}}{{jalink|浦和区|Q1154583}}{{jalink|岸町 (さいたま市)|Q11476004|岸町}}3-17-25 ; 交通アクセス * 鉄道：{{jalink|東日本旅客鉄道|Q499071}}（JR東日本）各線「{{jalink|浦和駅|Q652141}}」西口から徒歩約10分。 ; 周辺 * 調公園（つきのみやこうえん） *: 調神社境内に隣接し、さいたま市が所有する公園。{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}6年（{{jalink|1873年|Q7757}}）1月に、近代化路線の一環として公園制度を発足した明治政府の太政官が各府県に対して、寺社の境内地等名所・旧跡地に公園を設定することを通達し、明治7年（{{jalink|1874年|Q7761}}）2月に浦和公園として設置許可が下り、同年{{jalink|12月12日|Q2369}}に県下最初の公園である「浦和公園偕楽園」として設置された。桜の名所として春には「桜まつり」が開催される。 * {{jalink|埼玉県道213号曲本さいたま線|Q11426912}} *: 境内の西側を通る道路。{{jalink|白幡 (さいたま市)|Q17216551|白幡}}交差点 - 神社前 - {{jalink|浦和駅|Q652141}}西口交差点は{{jalink|五街道|Q1195921}}の1つである{{jalink|中山道|Q1154479}}のルートを踏襲しており、現在でも「旧中山道」の愛称が用いられている（「旧」が付くのは、後に{{jalink|国道17号|Q1057683}}が並行する形で開通したため）。神社付近から北は{{jalink|浦和宿|Q3552142}}になる。 (One theory) Around 2 years ({{jalink|771 years|Q29950}}) |Main shrine = {{jalink|Gongenzo|Q3155268}} |Alias = Chogu |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{jalink|July 20th|Q2728}} |Shinto ritual =|Map = {{Location map|Japan Saitama|lat_deg=35|lat_min=51|lat_sec=12.4|lon_deg=139|lon_min=39|lon_sec=21|label=Cho Shrine|position=top|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|51|12.4|N|139|39|21|E}}}} }} [[File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) entrance.JPG|thumb|right|{{Center|Precinct entrance}}{{Small|There is no torii gate, and a {{jalink|shimenawa|Q1887421}} is strung in its place. There are rabbits on both sides. }}]]'''Cho Shrine'' (Tsukijinja{{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}) is located in (Saitama City)|Q11476004|Kishimachi}} {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}} located in 3-chome. {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534|Old company rank}} is {{jalink|Prefectural company}}. Another name is ``Tsukinomiya''. Due to the ``Tsuki'' in the company name, there has been a belief in waiting for the moon since ancient times, and the shrine is known for having a guardian rabbit instead of {{jalink|guardian dog|Q20155846}}. === Enshrined God === The following three deities are enshrined. . * {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602|Amaterasu Omikami}} (Amaterasu Omikami) * {{jalink|Toyoukebime|Q2983724|Toyoukebime no Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo}} As a legend about the enshrined deity, one of the Seven Wonders ({{jalink|#Seven Wonders||described later}}) that has been handed down at Cho Shrine since ancient times is that there is no pine tree. According to the legend, there was an older sister god and a younger brother god in this area, but the younger god went to Omiya, and even though the older sister god was waiting for her to return, the younger brother didn't come back, so the older sister god didn't want to wait anymore. The older sister and younger gods that appear in this legend are said to correspond to Amaterasu Omikami (our company's enshrined deity) and Sujinson (Omiya's {{jalink|Hikawa Shrine|Q703633}} enshrined deity).According to other literature, the enshrined deity is {{jalink|Seoritsuhime|Q13579836|Seoritsuhima}}.{{efn|``{{jalink|Nihon Sokoku Fudoki}}'' includes the entry ``Enshrined Place Festival{{jalink|Seoritsuhime|Q13579836|Seoritsuhima}}ya''. }}, the theory that it is Chogyoku-no-Mikoto ``Musashikuni Shikinai Shijushi-jinja Shrine Meituke'', the theory that it is {{jalink|Tsukiyomi-no-Mikoto}} {{efn|``{{jalink|Edo Famous Places Encyclopedia|Q2704637}}'' is listed as ``Enshrined deity, {{jalink|Tsukiyomi no Mikoto}} Ichiza, Honji {{jalink|Seishi Bosatsu|Q1465683}}'' in our company's entry. }}, the theory that there are two pillars, the god of the sun and {{jalink|Uganomitama||Kura Inari Tama no Mikoto}}{{efn|``{{jalink|New version of Musashi Fudoki manuscript|Q11503943}} '', our company's section states, ``Our company is the place where the two shrines of the god of the sun, {{jalink|Uganomitama||Kura Inari Tama no Mikoto}}, are enshrined.'' Kishimura, Adachi District}}. }}, the theory that there are two pillars: {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602}} and {{jalink|Uganomitama||Ugaomitama}}{{jalink|Atsutane Hirata|Q2572749}} His works included ``Cho Shrine Research'' and ``{{jalink|Specially Selected Names of Gods|Q11571092}}''. === History === === Founding ===According to the company records (``Chogu Engi'' written in {{jalink|Kanbun|Q1146228}} 8th year ({{jalink|1668|Q7595}}), it was founded as a dedication shrine in March 1996 by the 9th {{jalink|Emperor Kaika|Q333548}}. Also, at the time of the 10th Emperor Sujin, {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}}, {{jalink|Wahime no Mikoto|Q4388732}, the chief priest of {{jalink|Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168}} }} visited the area, chose this area as a clean hill, and built a storehouse to store the offerings (tributes and offerings) to be presented to the Ise Grand Shrine. {{jalink|Kazusa Province|Q1055394|Kazusa}}・{{jalink|Shimousa Province|Q1053695|Shimousa}}・{{jalink|Awa Province|Q1053044|Awa}} , {{jalink|Ueno country|Q1054838|Ueno}}, {{jalink|Shimono country|Q1053699|Shimono}}), that is, it is determined to be the first-crop rice and tono collection center in the Kanto region. Furthermore, {{jalink|Hoki|Q18520676}}2 years ({{jalink|771 years|Q29950}}) {{jalink|June 20th (Lunar calendar)|Q839899|On June 20th}}, Fujiwara no Ason Tsunetoki held a dedication as {{jalink|Imperial envoy|Q11399920}}, so this was the regular festival (currently the new calendar {{ jalink|July 20th|Q2728}}) is said to have originated from {{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1976}}{{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Gods)|1984}}.The authenticity of the above tradition is uncertain, but since ``cho'' can be seen in the company name in the ``Shikinai-sha Investigation Report,'' etc., {{jalink|Imperial court (Japan)|Q30253177|It is assumed that the okura, where the offerings (tributes and gifts) to the Imperial Court}} were deposited, became the predecessor of Cho Shrine. A related legend is that one of the seven wonders of Cho Shrine is the absence of torii gates, and it is said that this is due to the removal of the torii gates and shrine gates that obstructed the transportation of treasures on the orders of {{jalink|Wahime no Mikoto|Q4388732}}, and this legend also indicates that the predecessor of Cho Shrine was Mikura.{{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1976}}. And in the second year of Houki (771), when it is said that there was a dedication ceremony, the affiliation of {{jalink|Musashi Province|Q907398}} changed from {{jalink|Tosando|Q1334011}} to {{jalink|Tokaido| Since it has been changed to Q1196306}}, "{{jalink|Shoku Nihongi|Q611399}}" October 2nd year of Houki (771) {{jalink|Kiu|Q1062557}} (27th) article. It is speculated that the actual founding of the shrine was when Ogura, which had ceased to be a place where Chomono could pass through due to this road change, was considered sacred and was dedicated as a shrine.Apart from the above, there is also a theory that it was dedicated by the Choren, Choshu, Chokishi, and Choi-sun clans (Cho clan) that appear in ``{{jalink|Newly Selected Surnames and Records|Q1045362}}'' and {{jalink|Rokukokushi|Q1151168|Kokushi}}. This Cho clan is an immigrant clan, and it has been pointed out that it is related to the fact that there are many {{jalink|immigrants|Q11561926}} living in groups in Togoku. Our company's location name "{{jalink|Kishimachi (Saitama City)|Q11476004|Kishi}}" is said to be derived from "{{jalink|Koshishi|Q55526932}}" {{Sfn|Kishimura (Heibonsha)|2004}}. In addition, there is a theory that associates the Japanese zelkova tree ({{jalink|zelkova|Q920344}}), which is often found in the temple grounds, with "tone"{{jalink|Kokugaku|Q855159 }}'s {{jalink|Harumura Kurokawa|Q6536184}} has a theory that ``There is an old Japanese tsuki tree called tsuki in the whole area of the shrine.Therefore, I think the key is a borrowed character for tsuki.'' Also {{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Great Dictionary of Shinto)|1941}}. === Outline === [[File:NDL-DC 1310959 Utagawa Kunisada Urawa Chonosha/Aburaya Okon crd.jpg|thumb|{{jalink|Utagawa Kunisada|Q467427}} Kiso Rokujuku's "Uragawa Chonosha/Aburaya Okon" (1852, 5th year of Kaei era)]] {{jalink|extension (Era name)|Q1327274|Extended}} "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) {{jalink|Engishiki divine name book|Q11064932|God name book}} So, in {{jalink|Musashi Province|Q907398}}{{jalink|Adachi District|Q11637033}}, "Cho Shrine" is written, and it is listed as {{jalink|Shikiuchi Shrine}}{{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}. From {{jalink|Middle Ages|Q12554}}, since ``cho'' has the same reading as ``moon'', it has come to be associated with the belief in waiting for the moon.{{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. {{jalink|Nobugen|Q1194159}}2nd year/{{jalink|Kenmu (Japan)|Q1193970|Kenmu}} In the 4th year ({{jalink|1337|Q6039}}), the shrine was reconstructed by {{jalink|Isshiki Noriyuki}} on the orders of {{jalink|Takauji Ashikaga|Q297107}}, but {{jal ink|Tensho|Q1145058}}It is said that it was burnt down by military fire during the {{jalink|Odawara Conquest|Q2622890}} in 18 years ({{jalink|1590|Q6764}}) {{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}.In {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, {{jalink|Keian|Q1146135}} 2nd year ({{jalink|1649|Q6945}}), {{jalink|Tokugawa Iemitsu|Q314481}} donated 7 stones of {{jalink|shuinchi}}. This red stamp letter says ``Tsukuyomisha,'' {{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}, but in "Chogu Engi" this company name is written incorrectly. However, this company name continues to be used in subsequent shuinjo. {{jalink|Betto-ji|Q11396755}} is Gassan-dera Temple, and {{jalink|Gyokuzo-in (Saitama City)|Q18336826|Gyokuzo-in}} was also attached to it. A twenty-three night hall was built in Gassan-ji Temple, and the {{jalink|Seishi Bosatsu|Q1465683}} of {{jalink|Getten|Q1061795|Tsukitenshi}} was enshrined as {{jalink|principal image|Q11520160}} {{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}.It is also listed in "{{jalink|Edo Meisho Zue|Q2704637}}," and many writers, writers, and artists also visit it, perhaps knowing about the god of Tsukimachi and Tsukuyomi. Among them, {{jalink|Kansei|Q1195131}} 3rd year ({{jalink|1791|Q7784}}) {{jalink|April 11th (Lunar Calendar)|Q838941|April 11th}}, the haiku poet {{jalink|Issa Kobayashi|Q312709}} wrote in his ``Kiko'' (Volume 5 of Issa's Complete Works) that he put his forehead to the ``Tsukiyomi no Miya'' located in the deep forest. In addition, {{jalink|Tsuda Daijo}}, a monk who loved traveling, wrote ``20 The shrine, which is called Sanya no Miya (omitted), has been constructed with great care and beauty, and the interior is decorated with so much heart and soul that it is filled with offerings that will astound your eyes if you wander into the middle of nowhere.'' {{Sfn|Saitama Shrines (Saitama Prefectural Shrine Agency)|1998}} Also, {{jalink|Culture (era name)|Q1373146|Bunka}}4 years ({{jalink|1807|Q6909}}) to {{jalink|Tenpo|Q1369045}}5 years ({{jalink|1834|Q759 1}}), the Tokugawa {{jalink|Shimizu family}} traveled to the suburbs of {{jalink|Edo|Q215646}} and visited {{jalink|Hiroshiki Yonin|Q11485425}}'s {{jalink|village}. In his records, Yasushi Omasa}} (whose name was Karyo) said, ``It is said to be a forest of {{jalink|Seishi Bosatsu|Q1465683}}.The head office and the hall of worship all have copper-roofed roofs, and the entire building is {{jalink|vermilion|Q737438| The structure of the shrine, which is painted vermilion and surrounded by azure (omitted), is not Buddhist, so it is likely that in ancient times it was worshiped as a god, but later it was worshiped as a Buddha.''Karyo Murao writes about the situation at that time. Author: Translated by Takatsugu Abe, “Edo Suburbs Roadside Signs: Modern Language Translation” {{jalink|Kodansha Academic Library|Q11632220}}, 2013. .In the 10th year of Tenpo ({{jalink|1839|Q7616}}), {{jalink|Atsutane Hirata|Q2572749}}, a {{jalink|Kokugaku|Q855159}} scholar, visited our company and wrote about the ``Chojinsha.'' In that book, we wrote that the shrine developed from a warehouse to store {{jalink|firstfruits|Q11396544}} to be delivered to {{jalink|Ise Jingu|Q687168}}. After {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}), {{jalink|Modern company ranking system|Q In 712534}}, it was ranked as {{jalink|gosha|Q134917316}}, and in 1898|Q7848}} it was promoted to {{jalink|kensha}}. === Precincts === The shrine building is {{jalink|Gongen-zukuri|Q3155268}}, with the main shrine and worship hall integrated, and was constructed in {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}}5 ({{jalink|1858|Q7684}}) {{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. The old main hall that was used until then is still used as the main hall of Inari Shrine within the shrine grounds. In addition, the shrine grounds within the shrine grounds form a forest of old Zelkova and Mukunoki trees, and have been designated as a natural monument by Saitama City as the ``Cho Shrine Precincts Forest.'' File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) kagura-den.JPG|Kaguraden File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) shin-ike.JPG|Kamiike no Usagi File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) chozusha.JPG|Rabbit of Chozuya File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) komausagi.JPG|Komausagi === Setsusha === [[File:Cho Shrine Old Main Hall (Inari Shrine).jpg|thumb|{{center|Inari Shrine}}{{small|The shrine building is the former main hall of Cho Shrine. Repairs were completed in 2017. The roof was changed from copper sheet to persimmon. }}
{{small|A cultural property designated by Saitama City. }}]] * Inari Shrine *: The shrine building is the former main hall of Chojinja Shrine. The roof is {{jalink|shingled||persimmon shingled}}. According to the ridge tag, it was built in {{jalink|Kyoho|Q1145048}}18 ({{jalink|1733|Q6972}}) and was used as the main shrine until the {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}} years ({{jalink|1854|Q7670}}-{{jalink|1860|Q7691}}). There are many carvings of rabbits, reflecting the Tsukimachi belief of Cho Shrine. This shrine building is designated as a cultural property by Saitama City . * Chogu Tenjin Shrine * Konpira Shrine Several other companies are located within the precincts. File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) keidaisha.JPG|{{small|Chogu Tenjin Shrine (front) and Konpira Shrine (back)}} [[File:Sushoson.jpg|Alternative sentence=Sushoson|Thumbnail|{{center|Sushoson Stone Statue}}]] === Festival === List of major festivals held annually at Cho Shrine [http://www.stib.jp/info/data/tsuki.html Cho Shrine] (Saitama Tourism and International Exchange Association, ``Saitama City Tourism and International Exchange Information''). . * Saitan Festival ({{jalink|January 1st|Q2150}}) * Setsubun Festival ({{jalink|February 3rd|Q2315}}) * Spring Festival ({{jalink|April 3rd|Q2509}}) * Great purification ceremony ({{jalink|June 30th|Q2657}}) * Shinko Festival ({{jalink|July 19th|Q2726}}) * '''Reitaisai'''' ({{jalink|July 20th|Q2728}}) - Follows the historic Hohei Day on June 20th of the lunar calendar, which is the origin of the Reitaisai. * Autumn Festival ({{jalink|November 23rd|Q3021}}) * Otoshi City Festival ({{jalink|Twelfth Day|Q22123263}}) ({{jalink|December 12th|Q2369}}) === Cultural assets === === Cultural property designated by Saitama City === * Tangible cultural properties{{PDFlink|[https://www.city.saitama.lg.jp/004/005/006/003/p001267_d/fil/bunkazaiitiran.pdf "List of designated and registered cultural properties in Saitama City"]}} (Saitama City homepage). ** Old main hall (with wooden tag {{jalink|Kyoho|Q1145048}} with an inscription of 18 years) (building) **: Edo period. {{jalink|Showa|Q327567}}53 ({{jalink|1978|Q2483}}) {{jalink|March 29|Q2459}} Designated . ** Thirty-six immortal paintings on a fan, 18 (paintings) **: Edo period. {{jalink|Thirty-six poets|Q671039|Thirty-six poets and paintings}} written in ink and paper on paper, of which 18 still exist. Dedicated in {{jalink|Kanbun|Q1146228}}9 ({{jalink|1669|Q7602}}). Designated on March 29, 1978 . ** Mikoshi phoenix (craft) **: Muromachi period. {{jalink|Phoenix|Q527525}} was attached to the top of {{jalink|Mikoshi|Q979940}}. Designated on March 29, 1978 . ** Cho Shrine plaque (calligraphy)**: Edo period. Zelkova wood {{jalink|flat plaque|Q5358873}}. The words ``Cho Shrine'' are written in block letters on the front. Written by {{jalink|Sadanobu Matsudaira|Q284038}} in 2nd year ({{jalink|1802|Q6883}}). Designated on March 29, 1978 . ** Chogu Engi (ancient document) **: Edo period. Selected by Jakudo the 12th {{jalink|Gyokuzoin (Saitama City)|Q18336826|Gyokuzoin}} in the 8th year of Kanbun ({{jalink|1668|Q7595}}). Designated on March 29, 1978 . * Natural monument ** Forest within the precincts of Cho Shrine **: Showa 45 ({{jalink|1970|Q2474}}) Designated {{jalink|March 10|Q2397}}. === Seven Wonders === At Cho Shrine, the following seven wonders are handed down. # No torii gate #: It is said that on the orders of {{jalink|Wahime no Mikoto|Q4388732}}, the shrine gates and torii gates, which were obstacles to the transportation of preparations, were removed. # No pine trees #: It is said that this name originates from the fact that there was an older sister god and a younger brother god in this area, but eventually the younger god went to Omiya and did not come back even though the older sister was waiting for him, so the older sister said that she did not want to wait any longer. It is also said that he was poked in the eye by a pine tree in the shrine grounds while his sister deity was waiting for him. # If you release a fish into Mitarai Pond (also known as Gourd Pond, which is now extinct), the fish will have one eye. # The rabbit is {{jalink|divine messenger|Q10444973}} # {{jalink|Nichiren|Q311478|Nichiren Saint}}Zelkova connecting pieces #: It is said that Nichiren, who was exiled to {{jalink|Sado Island|Q998623}}, tied a piece to a zelkova tree to pray for a safe birth for a woman there who was suffering from difficult childbirth. # No flies # No mosquitoes === Local information === ; Location * {{jalink|Saitama Prefecture|Q128186}}{{jalink|Saitama City|Q134635}}{{jalink|Urawa Ward|Q1154583}}{{jalink|Kishimachi (Saitama City)|Q11476004|Kishimachi}}3-17-25 ; Transportation access * Railway: {{jalink|East Japan Railway|Q499071}} (JR East)

link|Q11631781}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Cho Shrine |Image = [[File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) haiden.JPG|270px]]
House of worship |Location = {{jalink|Saitama Prefecture|Q128186}}{{jalink|Saitama City|Q134635}}{{jalink|Urawa Ward|Q1154583}}{{jalink|Kishimachi (Saitama City)|Q11476004|Kishimachi}}3-17-25 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 degrees minutes, seconds|35|51|12.4|N|139|39|21|E|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami}}

(One theory) Around 2 years ({{jalink|771 years|Q29950}}) |Main shrine = {{jalink|Gongenzo|Q3155268}} |Alias = Chogu |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{jalink|July 20th|Q2728}} |Shinto ritual =|Map = {{Location map|Japan Saitama|lat_deg=35|lat_min=51|lat_sec=12.4|lon_deg=139|lon_min=39|lon_sec=21|label=Cho Shrine|position=top|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|51|12.4|N|139|39|21|E}}}} }} [[File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) entrance.JPG|thumb|right|{{Center|Precinct entrance}}{{Small|There is no torii gate, and a {{jalink|shimenawa|Q1887421}} is strung in its place. There are rabbits on both sides. }}]]'''Cho Shrine'' (Tsukijinja{{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}) is located in (Saitama City)|Q11476004|Kishimachi}} {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}} located in 3-chome. {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534|Old company rank}} is {{jalink|Prefectural company}}. Another name is ``Tsukinomiya''. Due to the ``Tsuki'' in the company name, there has been a belief in waiting for the moon since ancient times, and the shrine is known for having a guardian rabbit instead of {{jalink|guardian dog|Q20155846}}. === Enshrined God === The following three deities are enshrined. . * {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602|Amaterasu Omikami}} (Amaterasu Omikami) * {{jalink|Toyoukebime|Q2983724|Toyoukebime no Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo}} As a legend about the enshrined deity, one of the Seven Wonders ({{jalink|#Seven Wonders||described later}}) that has been handed down at Cho Shrine since ancient times is that there is no pine tree. According to the legend, there was an older sister god and a younger brother god in this area, but the younger god went to Omiya, and even though the older sister god was waiting for her to return, the younger brother didn't come back, so the older sister god didn't want to wait anymore. The older sister and younger gods that appear in this legend are said to correspond to Amaterasu Omikami (our company's enshrined deity) and Sujinson (Omiya's {{jalink|Hikawa Shrine|Q703633}} enshrined deity).According to other literature, the enshrined deity is {{jalink|Seoritsuhime|Q13579836|Seoritsuhima}}.{{efn|``{{jalink|Nihon Sokoku Fudoki}}'' includes the entry ``Enshrined Place Festival{{jalink|Seoritsuhime|Q13579836|Seoritsuhima}}ya''. }}, the theory that it is Chogyoku-no-Mikoto ``Musashikuni Shikinai Shijushi-jinja Shrine Meituke'', the theory that it is {{jalink|Tsukiyomi-no-Mikoto}} {{efn|``{{jalink|Edo Famous Places Encyclopedia|Q2704637}}'' is listed as ``Enshrined deity, {{jalink|Tsukiyomi no Mikoto}} Ichiza, Honji {{jalink|Seishi Bosatsu|Q1465683}}'' in our company's entry. }}, the theory that there are two pillars, the god of the sun and {{jalink|Uganomitama||Kura Inari Tama no Mikoto}}{{efn|``{{jalink|New version of Musashi Fudoki manuscript|Q11503943}} '', our company's section states, ``Our company is the place where the two shrines of the god of the sun, {{jalink|Uganomitama||Kura Inari Tama no Mikoto}}, are enshrined.'' Kishimura, Adachi District}}. }}, the theory that there are two pillars: {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602}} and {{jalink|Uganomitama||Ugaomitama}}{{jalink|Atsutane Hirata|Q2572749}} His works included ``Cho Shrine Research'' and ``{{jalink|Specially Selected Names of Gods|Q11571092}}''. === History === === Founding ===According to the company records (``Chogu Engi'' written in {{jalink|Kanbun|Q1146228}} 8th year ({{jalink|1668|Q7595}}), it was founded as a dedication shrine in March 1996 by the 9th {{jalink|Emperor Kaika|Q333548}}. Also, at the time of the 10th Emperor Sujin, {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}}, {{jalink|Wahime no Mikoto|Q4388732}, the chief priest of {{jalink|Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168}} }} visited the area, chose this area as a clean hill, and built a storehouse to store the offerings (tributes and offerings) to be presented to the Ise Grand Shrine. {{jalink|Kazusa Province|Q1055394|Kazusa}}・{{jalink|Shimousa Province|Q1053695|Shimousa}}・{{jalink|Awa Province|Q1053044|Awa}} , {{jalink|Ueno country|Q1054838|Ueno}}, {{jalink|Shimono country|Q1053699|Shimono}}), that is, it is determined to be the first-crop rice and tono collection center in the Kanto region. Furthermore, {{jalink|Hoki|Q18520676}}2 years ({{jalink|771 years|Q29950}}) {{jalink|June 20th (Lunar calendar)|Q839899|On June 20th}}, Fujiwara no Ason Tsunetoki held a dedication as {{jalink|Imperial envoy|Q11399920}}, so this was the regular festival (currently the new calendar {{ jalink|July 20th|Q2728}}) is said to have originated from {{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1976}}{{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Gods)|1984}}.The authenticity of the above tradition is uncertain, but since ``cho'' can be seen in the company name in the ``Shikinai-sha Investigation Report,'' etc., {{jalink|Imperial court (Japan)|Q30253177|It is assumed that the okura, where the offerings (tributes and gifts) to the Imperial Court}} were deposited, became the predecessor of Cho Shrine. A related legend is that one of the seven wonders of Cho Shrine is the absence of torii gates, and it is said that this is due to the removal of the torii gates and shrine gates that obstructed the transportation of treasures on the orders of {{jalink|Wahime no Mikoto|Q4388732}}, and this legend also indicates that the predecessor of Cho Shrine was Mikura.{{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1976}}. And in the second year of Houki (771), when it is said that there was a dedication ceremony, the affiliation of {{jalink|Musashi Province|Q907398}} changed from {{jalink|Tosando|Q1334011}} to {{jalink|Tokaido| Since it has been changed to Q1196306}}, "{{jalink|Shoku Nihongi|Q611399}}" October 2nd year of Houki (771) {{jalink|Kiu|Q1062557}} (27th) article. It is speculated that the actual founding of the shrine was when Ogura, which had ceased to be a place where Chomono could pass through due to this road change, was considered sacred and was dedicated as a shrine.Apart from the above, there is also a theory that it was dedicated by the Choren, Choshu, Chokishi, and Choi-sun clans (Cho clan) that appear in ``{{jalink|Newly Selected Surnames and Records|Q1045362}}'' and {{jalink|Rokukokushi|Q1151168|Kokushi}}. This Cho clan is an immigrant clan, and it has been pointed out that it is related to the fact that there are many {{jalink|immigrants|Q11561926}} living in groups in Togoku. Our company's location name "{{jalink|Kishimachi (Saitama City)|Q11476004|Kishi}}" is said to be derived from "{{jalink|Koshishi|Q55526932}}" {{Sfn|Kishimura (Heibonsha)|2004}}. In addition, there is a theory that associates the Japanese zelkova tree ({{jalink|zelkova|Q920344}}), which is often found in the temple grounds, with "tone"{{jalink|Kokugaku|Q855159 }}'s {{jalink|Harumura Kurokawa|Q6536184}} has a theory that ``There is an old Japanese tsuki tree called tsuki in the whole area of the shrine.Therefore, I think the key is a borrowed character for tsuki.'' Also {{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Great Dictionary of Shinto)|1941}}. === Outline === [[File:NDL-DC 1310959 Utagawa Kunisada Urawa Chonosha/Aburaya Okon crd.jpg|thumb|{{jalink|Utagawa Kunisada|Q467427}} Kiso Rokujuku's "Uragawa Chonosha/Aburaya Okon" (1852, 5th year of Kaei era)]] {{jalink|extension (Era name)|Q1327274|Extended}} "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) {{jalink|Engishiki divine name book|Q11064932|God name book}} So, in {{jalink|Musashi Province|Q907398}}{{jalink|Adachi District|Q11637033}}, "Cho Shrine" is written, and it is listed as {{jalink|Shikiuchi Shrine}}{{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}. From {{jalink|Middle Ages|Q12554}}, since ``cho'' has the same reading as ``moon'', it has come to be associated with the belief in waiting for the moon.{{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. {{jalink|Nobugen|Q1194159}}2nd year/{{jalink|Kenmu (Japan)|Q1193970|Kenmu}} In the 4th year ({{jalink|1337|Q6039}}), the shrine was reconstructed by {{jalink|Isshiki Noriyuki}} on the orders of {{jalink|Takauji Ashikaga|Q297107}}, but {{jal ink|Tensho|Q1145058}}It is said that it was burnt down by military fire during the {{jalink|Odawara Conquest|Q2622890}} in 18 years ({{jalink|1590|Q6764}}) {{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}.In {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, {{jalink|Keian|Q1146135}} 2nd year ({{jalink|1649|Q6945}}), {{jalink|Tokugawa Iemitsu|Q314481}} donated 7 stones of {{jalink|shuinchi}}. This red stamp letter says ``Tsukuyomisha,'' {{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}, but in "Chogu Engi" this company name is written incorrectly. However, this company name continues to be used in subsequent shuinjo. {{jalink|Betto-ji|Q11396755}} is Gassan-dera Temple, and {{jalink|Gyokuzo-in (Saitama City)|Q18336826|Gyokuzo-in}} was also attached to it. A twenty-three night hall was built in Gassan-ji Temple, and the {{jalink|Seishi Bosatsu|Q1465683}} of {{jalink|Getten|Q1061795|Tsukitenshi}} was enshrined as {{jalink|principal image|Q11520160}} {{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}.It is also listed in "{{jalink|Edo Meisho Zue|Q2704637}}," and many writers, writers, and artists also visit it, perhaps knowing about the god of Tsukimachi and Tsukuyomi. Among them, {{jalink|Kansei|Q1195131}} 3rd year ({{jalink|1791|Q7784}}) {{jalink|April 11th (Lunar Calendar)|Q838941|April 11th}}, the haiku poet {{jalink|Issa Kobayashi|Q312709}} wrote in his ``Kiko'' (Volume 5 of Issa's Complete Works) that he put his forehead to the ``Tsukiyomi no Miya'' located in the deep forest. In addition, {{jalink|Tsuda Daijo}}, a monk who loved traveling, wrote ``20 The shrine, which is called Sanya no Miya (omitted), has been constructed with great care and beauty, and the interior is decorated with so much heart and soul that it is filled with offerings that will astound your eyes if you wander into the middle of nowhere.'' {{Sfn|Saitama Shrines (Saitama Prefectural Shrine Agency)|1998}} Also, {{jalink|Culture (era name)|Q1373146|Bunka}}4 years ({{jalink|1807|Q6909}}) to {{jalink|Tenpo|Q1369045}}5 years ({{jalink|1834|Q759 1}}), the Tokugawa {{jalink|Shimizu family}} traveled to the suburbs of {{jalink|Edo|Q215646}} and visited {{jalink|Hiroshiki Yonin|Q11485425}}'s {{jalink|village}. In his records, Yasushi Omasa}} (whose name was Karyo) said, ``It is said to be a forest of {{jalink|Seishi Bosatsu|Q1465683}}.The head office and the hall of worship all have copper-roofed roofs, and the entire building is {{jalink|vermilion|Q737438| The structure of the shrine, which is painted vermilion and surrounded by azure (omitted), is not Buddhist, so it is likely that in ancient times it was worshiped as a god, but later it was worshiped as a Buddha.''Karyo Murao writes about the situation at that time. Author: Translated by Takatsugu Abe, “Edo Suburbs Roadside Signs: Modern Language Translation” {{jalink|Kodansha Academic Library|Q11632220}}, 2013. .In the 10th year of Tenpo ({{jalink|1839|Q7616}}), {{jalink|Atsutane Hirata|Q2572749}}, a {{jalink|Kokugaku|Q855159}} scholar, visited our company and wrote about the ``Chojinsha.'' In that book, we wrote that the shrine developed from a warehouse to store {{jalink|firstfruits|Q11396544}} to be delivered to {{jalink|Ise Jingu|Q687168}}. After {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}), {{jalink|Modern company ranking system|Q In 712534}}, it was ranked as {{jalink|gosha|Q134917316}}, and in 1898|Q7848}} it was promoted to {{jalink|kensha}}. === Precincts === The shrine building is {{jalink|Gongen-zukuri|Q3155268}}, with the main shrine and worship hall integrated, and was constructed in {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}}5 ({{jalink|1858|Q7684}}) {{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. The old main hall that was used until then is still used as the main hall of Inari Shrine within the shrine grounds. In addition, the shrine grounds within the shrine grounds form a forest of old Zelkova and Mukunoki trees, and have been designated as a natural monument by Saitama City as the ``Cho Shrine Precincts Forest.'' File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) kagura-den.JPG|Kaguraden File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) shin-ike.JPG|Kamiike no Usagi File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) chozusha.JPG|Rabbit of Chozuya File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) komausagi.JPG|Komausagi === Setsusha === [[File:Cho Shrine Old Main Hall (Inari Shrine).jpg|thumb|{{center|Inari Shrine}}{{small|The shrine building is the former main hall of Cho Shrine. Repairs were completed in 2017. The roof was changed from copper sheet to persimmon. }}
{{small|A cultural property designated by Saitama City. }}]] * Inari Shrine *: The shrine building is the former main hall of Chojinja Shrine. The roof is {{jalink|shingled||persimmon shingled}}. According to the ridge tag, it was built in {{jalink|Kyoho|Q1145048}}18 ({{jalink|1733|Q6972}}) and was used as the main shrine until the {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}} years ({{jalink|1854|Q7670}}-{{jalink|1860|Q7691}}). There are many carvings of rabbits, reflecting the Tsukimachi belief of Cho Shrine. This shrine building is designated as a cultural property by Saitama City . * Chogu Tenjin Shrine * Konpira Shrine Several other companies are located within the precincts. File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) keidaisha.JPG|{{small|Chogu Tenjin Shrine (front) and Konpira Shrine (back)}} [[File:Sushoson.jpg|Alternative sentence=Sushoson|Thumbnail|{{center|Sushoson Stone Statue}}]] === Festival === List of major festivals held annually at Cho Shrine [http://www.stib.jp/info/data/tsuki.html Cho Shrine] (Saitama Tourism and International Exchange Association, ``Saitama City Tourism and International Exchange Information''). . * Saitan Festival ({{jalink|January 1st|Q2150}}) * Setsubun Festival ({{jalink|February 3rd|Q2315}}) * Spring Festival ({{jalink|April 3rd|Q2509}}) * Great purification ceremony ({{jalink|June 30th|Q2657}}) * Shinko Festival ({{jalink|July 19th|Q2726}}) * '''Reitaisai'''' ({{jalink|July 20th|Q2728}}) - Follows the historic Hohei Day on June 20th of the lunar calendar, which is the origin of the Reitaisai. * Autumn Festival ({{jalink|November 23rd|Q3021}}) * Otoshi City Festival ({{jalink|Twelfth Day|Q22123263}}) ({{jalink|December 12th|Q2369}}) === Cultural assets === === Cultural property designated by Saitama City === * Tangible cultural properties{{PDFlink|[https://www.city.saitama.lg.jp/004/005/006/003/p001267_d/fil/bunkazaiitiran.pdf "List of designated and registered cultural properties in Saitama City"]}} (Saitama City homepage). ** Old main hall (with wooden tag {{jalink|Kyoho|Q1145048}} with an inscription of 18 years) (building) **: Edo period. {{jalink|Showa|Q327567}}53 ({{jalink|1978|Q2483}}) {{jalink|March 29|Q2459}} Designated . ** Thirty-six immortal paintings on a fan, 18 (paintings) **: Edo period. {{jalink|Thirty-six poets|Q671039|Thirty-six poets and paintings}} written in ink and paper on paper, of which 18 still exist. Dedicated in {{jalink|Kanbun|Q1146228}}9 ({{jalink|1669|Q7602}}). Designated on March 29, 1978 . ** Mikoshi phoenix (craft) **: Muromachi period. {{jalink|Phoenix|Q527525}} was attached to the top of {{jalink|Mikoshi|Q979940}}. Designated on March 29, 1978 . ** Cho Shrine plaque (calligraphy)**: Edo period. Zelkova wood {{jalink|flat plaque|Q5358873}}. The words ``Cho Shrine'' are written in block letters on the front. Written by {{jalink|Sadanobu Matsudaira|Q284038}} in 2nd year ({{jalink|1802|Q6883}}). Designated on March 29, 1978 . ** Chogu Engi (ancient document) **: Edo period. Selected by Jakudo the 12th {{jalink|Gyokuzoin (Saitama City)|Q18336826|Gyokuzoin}} in the 8th year of Kanbun ({{jalink|1668|Q7595}}). Designated on March 29, 1978 . * Natural monument ** Forest within the precincts of Cho Shrine **: Showa 45 ({{jalink|1970|Q2474}}) Designated {{jalink|March 10|Q2397}}. === Seven Wonders === At Cho Shrine, the following seven wonders are handed down. # No torii gate #: It is said that on the orders of {{jalink|Wahime no Mikoto|Q4388732}}, the shrine gates and torii gates, which were obstacles to the transportation of preparations, were removed. # No pine trees #: It is said that this name originates from the fact that there was an older sister god and a younger brother god in this area, but eventually the younger god went to Omiya and did not come back even though the older sister was waiting for him, so the older sister said that she did not want to wait any longer. It is also said that he was poked in the eye by a pine tree in the shrine grounds while his sister deity was waiting for him. # If you release a fish into Mitarai Pond (also known as Gourd Pond, which is now extinct), the fish will have one eye. # The rabbit is {{jalink|divine messenger|Q10444973}} # {{jalink|Nichiren|Q311478|Nichiren Saint}}Zelkova connecting pieces #: It is said that Nichiren, who was exiled to {{jalink|Sado Island|Q998623}}, tied a piece to a zelkova tree to pray for a safe birth for a woman there who was suffering from difficult childbirth. # No flies # No mosquitoes === Local information === ; Location * {{jalink|Saitama Prefecture|Q128186}}{{jalink|Saitama City|Q134635}}{{jalink|Urawa Ward|Q1154583}}{{jalink|Kishimachi (Saitama City)|Q11476004|Kishimachi}}3-17-25 ; Transportation access * Railway: {{jalink|East Japan Railway|Q499071}} (JR East)

link|Q11631781}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Cho Shrine |Image = [[File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) haiden.JPG|270px]]
House of worship |Location = {{jalink|Saitama Prefecture|Q128186}}{{jalink|Saitama City|Q134635}}{{jalink|Urawa Ward|Q1154583}}{{jalink|Kishimachi (Saitama City)|Q11476004|Kishimachi}}3-17-25 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 degrees minutes, seconds|35|51|12.4|N|139|39|21|E|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami}}

Old {{jalink|Prefectural company}} | Founded = (tradition) 9th {{jalink|Emperor Kaika|Q333548}} Based on
(One theory) Around 2 years ({{jalink|771 years|Q29950}}) |Main shrine = {{jalink|Gongenzo|Q3155268}} |Alias = Chogu |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{jalink|July 20th|Q2728}} |Shinto ritual =|Map = {{Location map|Japan Saitama|lat_deg=35|lat_min=51|lat_sec=12.4|lon_deg=139|lon_min=39|lon_sec=21|label=Cho Shrine|position=top|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|51|12.4|N|139|39|21|E}}}} }} [[File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) entrance.JPG|thumb|right|{{Center|Precinct entrance}}{{Small|There is no torii gate, and a {{jalink|shimenawa|Q1887421}} is strung in its place. There are rabbits on both sides. }}]]'''Cho Shrine'' (Tsukijinja{{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}) is located in (Saitama City)|Q11476004|Kishimachi}} {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}} located in 3-chome. {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534|Old company rank}} is {{jalink|Prefectural company}}. Another name is ``Tsukinomiya''. Due to the ``Tsuki'' in the company name, there has been a belief in waiting for the moon since ancient times, and the shrine is known for having a guardian rabbit instead of {{jalink|guardian dog|Q20155846}}. === Enshrined God === The following three deities are enshrined. . * {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602|Amaterasu Omikami}} (Amaterasu Omikami) * {{jalink|Toyoukebime|Q2983724|Toyoukebime no Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo}} As a legend about the enshrined deity, one of the Seven Wonders ({{jalink|#Seven Wonders||described later}}) that has been handed down at Cho Shrine since ancient times is that there is no pine tree. According to the legend, there was an older sister god and a younger brother god in this area, but the younger god went to Omiya, and even though the older sister god was waiting for her to return, the younger brother didn't come back, so the older sister god didn't want to wait anymore. The older sister and younger gods that appear in this legend are said to correspond to Amaterasu Omikami (our company's enshrined deity) and Sujinson (Omiya's {{jalink|Hikawa Shrine|Q703633}} enshrined deity).According to other literature, the enshrined deity is {{jalink|Seoritsuhime|Q13579836|Seoritsuhima}}.{{efn|``{{jalink|Nihon Sokoku Fudoki}}'' includes the entry ``Enshrined Place Festival{{jalink|Seoritsuhime|Q13579836|Seoritsuhima}}ya''. }}, the theory that it is Chogyoku-no-Mikoto ``Musashikuni Shikinai Shijushi-jinja Shrine Meituke'', the theory that it is {{jalink|Tsukiyomi-no-Mikoto}} {{efn|``{{jalink|Edo Famous Places Encyclopedia|Q2704637}}'' is listed as ``Enshrined deity, {{jalink|Tsukiyomi no Mikoto}} Ichiza, Honji {{jalink|Seishi Bosatsu|Q1465683}}'' in our company's entry. }}, the theory that there are two pillars, the god of the sun and {{jalink|Uganomitama||Kura Inari Tama no Mikoto}}{{efn|``{{jalink|New version of Musashi Fudoki manuscript|Q11503943}} '', our company's section states, ``Our company is the place where the two shrines of the god of the sun, {{jalink|Uganomitama||Kura Inari Tama no Mikoto}}, are enshrined.'' Kishimura, Adachi District}}. }}, the theory that there are two pillars: {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602}} and {{jalink|Uganomitama||Ugaomitama}}{{jalink|Atsutane Hirata|Q2572749}} His works included ``Cho Shrine Research'' and ``{{jalink|Specially Selected Names of Gods|Q11571092}}''. === History === === Founding ===According to the company records (``Chogu Engi'' written in {{jalink|Kanbun|Q1146228}} 8th year ({{jalink|1668|Q7595}}), it was founded as a dedication shrine in March 1996 by the 9th {{jalink|Emperor Kaika|Q333548}}. Also, at the time of the 10th Emperor Sujin, {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}}, {{jalink|Wahime no Mikoto|Q4388732}, the chief priest of {{jalink|Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168}} }} visited the area, chose this area as a clean hill, and built a storehouse to store the offerings (tributes and offerings) to be presented to the Ise Grand Shrine. {{jalink|Kazusa Province|Q1055394|Kazusa}}・{{jalink|Shimousa Province|Q1053695|Shimousa}}・{{jalink|Awa Province|Q1053044|Awa}} , {{jalink|Ueno country|Q1054838|Ueno}}, {{jalink|Shimono country|Q1053699|Shimono}}), that is, it is determined to be the first-crop rice and tono collection center in the Kanto region. Furthermore, {{jalink|Hoki|Q18520676}}2 years ({{jalink|771 years|Q29950}}) {{jalink|June 20th (Lunar calendar)|Q839899|On June 20th}}, Fujiwara no Ason Tsunetoki held a dedication as {{jalink|Imperial envoy|Q11399920}}, so this was the regular festival (currently the new calendar {{ jalink|July 20th|Q2728}}) is said to have originated from {{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1976}}{{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Gods)|1984}}.The authenticity of the above tradition is uncertain, but since ``cho'' can be seen in the company name in the ``Shikinai-sha Investigation Report,'' etc., {{jalink|Imperial court (Japan)|Q30253177|It is assumed that the okura, where the offerings (tributes and gifts) to the Imperial Court}} were deposited, became the predecessor of Cho Shrine. A related legend is that one of the seven wonders of Cho Shrine is the absence of torii gates, and it is said that this is due to the removal of the torii gates and shrine gates that obstructed the transportation of treasures on the orders of {{jalink|Wahime no Mikoto|Q4388732}}, and this legend also indicates that the predecessor of Cho Shrine was Mikura.{{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1976}}. And in the second year of Houki (771), when it is said that there was a dedication ceremony, the affiliation of {{jalink|Musashi Province|Q907398}} changed from {{jalink|Tosando|Q1334011}} to {{jalink|Tokaido| Since it has been changed to Q1196306}}, "{{jalink|Shoku Nihongi|Q611399}}" October 2nd year of Houki (771) {{jalink|Kiu|Q1062557}} (27th) article. It is speculated that the actual founding of the shrine was when Ogura, which had ceased to be a place where Chomono could pass through due to this road change, was considered sacred and was dedicated as a shrine.Apart from the above, there is also a theory that it was dedicated by the Choren, Choshu, Chokishi, and Choi-sun clans (Cho clan) that appear in ``{{jalink|Newly Selected Surnames and Records|Q1045362}}'' and {{jalink|Rokukokushi|Q1151168|Kokushi}}. This Cho clan is an immigrant clan, and it has been pointed out that it is related to the fact that there are many {{jalink|immigrants|Q11561926}} living in groups in Togoku. Our company's location name "{{jalink|Kishimachi (Saitama City)|Q11476004|Kishi}}" is said to be derived from "{{jalink|Koshishi|Q55526932}}" {{Sfn|Kishimura (Heibonsha)|2004}}. In addition, there is a theory that associates the Japanese zelkova tree ({{jalink|zelkova|Q920344}}), which is often found in the temple grounds, with "tone"{{jalink|Kokugaku|Q855159 }}'s {{jalink|Harumura Kurokawa|Q6536184}} has a theory that ``There is an old Japanese tsuki tree called tsuki in the whole area of the shrine.Therefore, I think the key is a borrowed character for tsuki.'' Also {{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Great Dictionary of Shinto)|1941}}. === Outline === [[File:NDL-DC 1310959 Utagawa Kunisada Urawa Chonosha/Aburaya Okon crd.jpg|thumb|{{jalink|Utagawa Kunisada|Q467427}} Kiso Rokujuku's "Uragawa Chonosha/Aburaya Okon" (1852, 5th year of Kaei era)]] {{jalink|extension (Era name)|Q1327274|Extended}} "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) {{jalink|Engishiki divine name book|Q11064932|God name book}} So, in {{jalink|Musashi Province|Q907398}}{{jalink|Adachi District|Q11637033}}, "Cho Shrine" is written, and it is listed as {{jalink|Shikiuchi Shrine}}{{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}. From {{jalink|Middle Ages|Q12554}}, since ``cho'' has the same reading as ``moon'', it has come to be associated with the belief in waiting for the moon.{{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. {{jalink|Nobugen|Q1194159}}2nd year/{{jalink|Kenmu (Japan)|Q1193970|Kenmu}} In the 4th year ({{jalink|1337|Q6039}}), the shrine was reconstructed by {{jalink|Isshiki Noriyuki}} on the orders of {{jalink|Takauji Ashikaga|Q297107}}, but {{jal ink|Tensho|Q1145058}}It is said that it was burnt down by military fire during the {{jalink|Odawara Conquest|Q2622890}} in 18 years ({{jalink|1590|Q6764}}) {{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}.In {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, {{jalink|Keian|Q1146135}} 2nd year ({{jalink|1649|Q6945}}), {{jalink|Tokugawa Iemitsu|Q314481}} donated 7 stones of {{jalink|shuinchi}}. This red stamp letter says ``Tsukuyomisha,'' {{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}, but in "Chogu Engi" this company name is written incorrectly. However, this company name continues to be used in subsequent shuinjo. {{jalink|Betto-ji|Q11396755}} is Gassan-dera Temple, and {{jalink|Gyokuzo-in (Saitama City)|Q18336826|Gyokuzo-in}} was also attached to it. A twenty-three night hall was built in Gassan-ji Temple, and the {{jalink|Seishi Bosatsu|Q1465683}} of {{jalink|Getten|Q1061795|Tsukitenshi}} was enshrined as {{jalink|principal image|Q11520160}} {{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}.It is also listed in "{{jalink|Edo Meisho Zue|Q2704637}}," and many writers, writers, and artists also visit it, perhaps knowing about the god of Tsukimachi and Tsukuyomi. Among them, {{jalink|Kansei|Q1195131}} 3rd year ({{jalink|1791|Q7784}}) {{jalink|April 11th (Lunar Calendar)|Q838941|April 11th}}, the haiku poet {{jalink|Issa Kobayashi|Q312709}} wrote in his ``Kiko'' (Volume 5 of Issa's Complete Works) that he put his forehead to the ``Tsukiyomi no Miya'' located in the deep forest. In addition, {{jalink|Tsuda Daijo}}, a monk who loved traveling, wrote ``20 The shrine, which is called Sanya no Miya (omitted), has been constructed with great care and beauty, and the interior is decorated with so much heart and soul that it is filled with offerings that will astound your eyes if you wander into the middle of nowhere.'' {{Sfn|Saitama Shrines (Saitama Prefectural Shrine Agency)|1998}} Also, {{jalink|Culture (era name)|Q1373146|Bunka}}4 years ({{jalink|1807|Q6909}}) to {{jalink|Tenpo|Q1369045}}5 years ({{jalink|1834|Q759 1}}), the Tokugawa {{jalink|Shimizu family}} traveled to the suburbs of {{jalink|Edo|Q215646}} and visited {{jalink|Hiroshiki Yonin|Q11485425}}'s {{jalink|village}. In his records, Yasushi Omasa}} (whose name was Karyo) said, ``It is said to be a forest of {{jalink|Seishi Bosatsu|Q1465683}}.The head office and the hall of worship all have copper-roofed roofs, and the entire building is {{jalink|vermilion|Q737438| The structure of the shrine, which is painted vermilion and surrounded by azure (omitted), is not Buddhist, so it is likely that in ancient times it was worshiped as a god, but later it was worshiped as a Buddha.''Karyo Murao writes about the situation at that time. Author: Translated by Takatsugu Abe, “Edo Suburbs Roadside Signs: Modern Language Translation” {{jalink|Kodansha Academic Library|Q11632220}}, 2013. .In the 10th year of Tenpo ({{jalink|1839|Q7616}}), {{jalink|Atsutane Hirata|Q2572749}}, a {{jalink|Kokugaku|Q855159}} scholar, visited our company and wrote about the ``Chojinsha.'' In that book, we wrote that the shrine developed from a warehouse to store {{jalink|firstfruits|Q11396544}} to be delivered to {{jalink|Ise Jingu|Q687168}}. After {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}), {{jalink|Modern company ranking system|Q In 712534}}, it was ranked as {{jalink|gosha|Q134917316}}, and in 1898|Q7848}} it was promoted to {{jalink|kensha}}. === Precincts === The shrine building is {{jalink|Gongen-zukuri|Q3155268}}, with the main shrine and worship hall integrated, and was constructed in {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}}5 ({{jalink|1858|Q7684}}) {{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. The old main hall that was used until then is still used as the main hall of Inari Shrine within the shrine grounds. In addition, the shrine grounds within the shrine grounds form a forest of old Zelkova and Mukunoki trees, and have been designated as a natural monument by Saitama City as the ``Cho Shrine Precincts Forest.'' File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) kagura-den.JPG|Kaguraden File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) shin-ike.JPG|Kamiike no Usagi File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) chozusha.JPG|Rabbit of Chozuya File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) komausagi.JPG|Komausagi === Setsusha === [[File:Cho Shrine Old Main Hall (Inari Shrine).jpg|thumb|{{center|Inari Shrine}}{{small|The shrine building is the former main hall of Cho Shrine. Repairs were completed in 2017. The roof was changed from copper sheet to persimmon. }}
{{small|A cultural property designated by Saitama City. }}]] * Inari Shrine *: The shrine building is the former main hall of Chojinja Shrine. The roof is {{jalink|shingled||persimmon shingled}}. According to the ridge tag, it was built in {{jalink|Kyoho|Q1145048}}18 ({{jalink|1733|Q6972}}) and was used as the main shrine until the {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}} years ({{jalink|1854|Q7670}}-{{jalink|1860|Q7691}}). There are many carvings of rabbits, reflecting the Tsukimachi belief of Cho Shrine. This shrine building is designated as a cultural property by Saitama City . * Chogu Tenjin Shrine * Konpira Shrine Several other companies are located within the precincts. File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) keidaisha.JPG|{{small|Chogu Tenjin Shrine (front) and Konpira Shrine (back)}} [[File:Sushoson.jpg|Alternative sentence=Sushoson|Thumbnail|{{center|Sushoson Stone Statue}}]] === Festival === List of major festivals held annually at Cho Shrine [http://www.stib.jp/info/data/tsuki.html Cho Shrine] (Saitama Tourism and International Exchange Association, ``Saitama City Tourism and International Exchange Information''). . * Saitan Festival ({{jalink|January 1st|Q2150}}) * Setsubun Festival ({{jalink|February 3rd|Q2315}}) * Spring Festival ({{jalink|April 3rd|Q2509}}) * Great purification ceremony ({{jalink|June 30th|Q2657}}) * Shinko Festival ({{jalink|July 19th|Q2726}}) * '''Reitaisai'''' ({{jalink|July 20th|Q2728}}) - Follows the historic Hohei Day on June 20th of the lunar calendar, which is the origin of the Reitaisai. * Autumn Festival ({{jalink|November 23rd|Q3021}}) * Otoshi City Festival ({{jalink|Twelfth Day|Q22123263}}) ({{jalink|December 12th|Q2369}}) === Cultural assets === === Cultural property designated by Saitama City === * Tangible cultural properties{{PDFlink|[https://www.city.saitama.lg.jp/004/005/006/003/p001267_d/fil/bunkazaiitiran.pdf "List of designated and registered cultural properties in Saitama City"]}} (Saitama City homepage). ** Old main hall (with wooden tag {{jalink|Kyoho|Q1145048}} with an inscription of 18 years) (building) **: Edo period. {{jalink|Showa|Q327567}}53 ({{jalink|1978|Q2483}}) {{jalink|March 29|Q2459}} Designated . ** Thirty-six immortal paintings on a fan, 18 (paintings) **: Edo period. {{jalink|Thirty-six poets|Q671039|Thirty-six poets and paintings}} written in ink and paper on paper, of which 18 still exist. Dedicated in {{jalink|Kanbun|Q1146228}}9 ({{jalink|1669|Q7602}}). Designated on March 29, 1978 . ** Mikoshi phoenix (craft) **: Muromachi period. {{jalink|Phoenix|Q527525}} was attached to the top of {{jalink|Mikoshi|Q979940}}. Designated on March 29, 1978 . ** Cho Shrine plaque (calligraphy)**: Edo period. Zelkova wood {{jalink|flat plaque|Q5358873}}. The words ``Cho Shrine'' are written in block letters on the front. Written by {{jalink|Sadanobu Matsudaira|Q284038}} in 2nd year ({{jalink|1802|Q6883}}). Designated on March 29, 1978 . ** Chogu Engi (ancient document) **: Edo period. Selected by Jakudo the 12th {{jalink|Gyokuzoin (Saitama City)|Q18336826|Gyokuzoin}} in the 8th year of Kanbun ({{jalink|1668|Q7595}}). Designated on March 29, 1978 . * Natural monument ** Forest within the precincts of Cho Shrine **: Showa 45 ({{jalink|1970|Q2474}}) Designated {{jalink|March 10|Q2397}}. === Seven Wonders === At Cho Shrine, the following seven wonders are handed down. # No torii gate #: It is said that on the orders of {{jalink|Wahime no Mikoto|Q4388732}}, the shrine gates and torii gates, which were obstacles to the transportation of preparations, were removed. # No pine trees #: It is said that this name originates from the fact that there was an older sister god and a younger brother god in this area, but eventually the younger god went to Omiya and did not come back even though the older sister was waiting for him, so the older sister said that she did not want to wait any longer. It is also said that he was poked in the eye by a pine tree in the shrine grounds while his sister deity was waiting for him. # If you release a fish into Mitarai Pond (also known as Gourd Pond, which is now extinct), the fish will have one eye. # The rabbit is {{jalink|divine messenger|Q10444973}} # {{jalink|Nichiren|Q311478|Nichiren Saint}}Zelkova connecting pieces #: It is said that Nichiren, who was exiled to {{jalink|Sado Island|Q998623}}, tied a piece to a zelkova tree to pray for a safe birth for a woman there who was suffering from difficult childbirth. # No flies # No mosquitoes === Local information === ; Location * {{jalink|Saitama Prefecture|Q128186}}{{jalink|Saitama City|Q134635}}{{jalink|Urawa Ward|Q1154583}}{{jalink|Kishimachi (Saitama City)|Q11476004|Kishimachi}}3-17-25 ; Transportation access * Railway: {{jalink|East Japan Railway|Q499071}} (JR East)

There are rabbits on both sides. }}]]'''Cho Shrine'' (Tsukijinja{{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}) is located in (Saitama City)|Q11476004|Kishimachi}} {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}} located in 3-chome. {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534|Old company rank}} is {{jalink|Prefectural company}}. Another name is ``Tsukinomiya''. Due to the ``Tsuki'' in the company name, there has been a belief in waiting for the moon since ancient times, and the shrine is known for having a guardian rabbit instead of {{jalink|guardian dog|Q20155846}}. === Enshrined God === The following three deities are enshrined. . * {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602|Amaterasu Omikami}} (Amaterasu Omikami) * {{jalink|Toyoukebime|Q2983724|Toyoukebime no Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo}} As a legend about the enshrined deity, one of the Seven Wonders ({{jalink|#Seven Wonders||described later}}) that has been handed down at Cho Shrine since ancient times is that there is no pine tree. According to the legend, there was an older sister god and a younger brother god in this area, but the younger god went to Omiya, and even though the older sister god was waiting for her to return, the younger brother didn't come back, so the older sister god didn't want to wait anymore. The older sister and younger gods that appear in this legend are said to correspond to Amaterasu Omikami (our company's enshrined deity) and Sujinson (Omiya's {{jalink|Hikawa Shrine|Q703633}} enshrined deity).According to other literature, the enshrined deity is {{jalink|Seoritsuhime|Q13579836|Seoritsuhima}}.{{efn|``{{jalink|Nihon Sokoku Fudoki}}'' includes the entry ``Enshrined Place Festival{{jalink|Seoritsuhime|Q13579836|Seoritsuhima}}ya''. }}, the theory that it is Chogyoku-no-Mikoto ``Musashikuni Shikinai Shijushi-jinja Shrine Meituke'', the theory that it is {{jalink|Tsukiyomi-no-Mikoto}} {{efn|``{{jalink|Edo Famous Places Encyclopedia|Q2704637}}'' is listed as ``Enshrined deity, {{jalink|Tsukiyomi no Mikoto}} Ichiza, Honji {{jalink|Seishi Bosatsu|Q1465683}}'' in our company's entry. }}, the theory that there are two pillars, the god of the sun and {{jalink|Uganomitama||Kura Inari Tama no Mikoto}}{{efn|``{{jalink|New version of Musashi Fudoki manuscript|Q11503943}} '', our company's section states, ``Our company is the place where the two shrines of the god of the sun, {{jalink|Uganomitama||Kura Inari Tama no Mikoto}}, are enshrined.'' Kishimura, Adachi District}}. }}, the theory that there are two pillars: {{jalink|Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602}} and {{jalink|Uganomitama||Ugaomitama}}{{jalink|Atsutane Hirata|Q2572749}} His works included ``Cho Shrine Research'' and ``{{jalink|Specially Selected Names of Gods|Q11571092}}''. === History === === Founding ===According to the company records (``Chogu Engi'' written in {{jalink|Kanbun|Q1146228}} 8th year ({{jalink|1668|Q7595}}), it was founded as a dedication shrine in March 1996 by the 9th {{jalink|Emperor Kaika|Q333548}}. Also, at the time of the 10th Emperor Sujin, {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}}, {{jalink|Wahime no Mikoto|Q4388732}, the chief priest of {{jalink|Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168}} }} visited the area, chose this area as a clean hill, and built a storehouse to store the offerings (tributes and offerings) to be presented to the Ise Grand Shrine. {{jalink|Kazusa Province|Q1055394|Kazusa}}・{{jalink|Shimousa Province|Q1053695|Shimousa}}・{{jalink|Awa Province|Q1053044|Awa}} , {{jalink|Ueno country|Q1054838|Ueno}}, {{jalink|Shimono country|Q1053699|Shimono}}), that is, it is determined to be the first-crop rice and tono collection center in the Kanto region. Furthermore, {{jalink|Hoki|Q18520676}}2 years ({{jalink|771 years|Q29950}}) {{jalink|June 20th (Lunar calendar)|Q839899|On June 20th}}, Fujiwara no Ason Tsunetoki held a dedication as {{jalink|Imperial envoy|Q11399920}}, so this was the regular festival (currently the new calendar {{ jalink|July 20th|Q2728}}) is said to have originated from {{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1976}}{{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Gods)|1984}}.The authenticity of the above tradition is uncertain, but since ``cho'' can be seen in the company name in the ``Shikinai-sha Investigation Report,'' etc., {{jalink|Imperial court (Japan)|Q30253177|It is assumed that the okura, where the offerings (tributes and gifts) to the Imperial Court}} were deposited, became the predecessor of Cho Shrine. A related legend is that one of the seven wonders of Cho Shrine is the absence of torii gates, and it is said that this is due to the removal of the torii gates and shrine gates that obstructed the transportation of treasures on the orders of {{jalink|Wahime no Mikoto|Q4388732}}, and this legend also indicates that the predecessor of Cho Shrine was Mikura.{{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1976}}. And in the second year of Houki (771), when it is said that there was a dedication ceremony, the affiliation of {{jalink|Musashi Province|Q907398}} changed from {{jalink|Tosando|Q1334011}} to {{jalink|Tokaido| Since it has been changed to Q1196306}}, "{{jalink|Shoku Nihongi|Q611399}}" October 2nd year of Houki (771) {{jalink|Kiu|Q1062557}} (27th) article. It is speculated that the actual founding of the shrine was when Ogura, which had ceased to be a place where Chomono could pass through due to this road change, was considered sacred and was dedicated as a shrine.Apart from the above, there is also a theory that it was dedicated by the Choren, Choshu, Chokishi, and Choi-sun clans (Cho clan) that appear in ``{{jalink|Newly Selected Surnames and Records|Q1045362}}'' and {{jalink|Rokukokushi|Q1151168|Kokushi}}. This Cho clan is an immigrant clan, and it has been pointed out that it is related to the fact that there are many {{jalink|immigrants|Q11561926}} living in groups in Togoku. Our company's location name "{{jalink|Kishimachi (Saitama City)|Q11476004|Kishi}}" is said to be derived from "{{jalink|Koshishi|Q55526932}}" {{Sfn|Kishimura (Heibonsha)|2004}}. In addition, there is a theory that associates the Japanese zelkova tree ({{jalink|zelkova|Q920344}}), which is often found in the temple grounds, with "tone"{{jalink|Kokugaku|Q855159 }}'s {{jalink|Harumura Kurokawa|Q6536184}} has a theory that ``There is an old Japanese tsuki tree called tsuki in the whole area of the shrine.Therefore, I think the key is a borrowed character for tsuki.'' Also {{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Great Dictionary of Shinto)|1941}}. === Outline === [[File:NDL-DC 1310959 Utagawa Kunisada Urawa Chonosha/Aburaya Okon crd.jpg|thumb|{{jalink|Utagawa Kunisada|Q467427}} Kiso Rokujuku's "Uragawa Chonosha/Aburaya Okon" (1852, 5th year of Kaei era)]] {{jalink|extension (Era name)|Q1327274|Extended}} "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) {{jalink|Engishiki divine name book|Q11064932|God name book}} So, in {{jalink|Musashi Province|Q907398}}{{jalink|Adachi District|Q11637033}}, "Cho Shrine" is written, and it is listed as {{jalink|Shikiuchi Shrine}}{{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}. From {{jalink|Middle Ages|Q12554}}, since ``cho'' has the same reading as ``moon'', it has come to be associated with the belief in waiting for the moon.{{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. {{jalink|Nobugen|Q1194159}}2nd year/{{jalink|Kenmu (Japan)|Q1193970|Kenmu}} In the 4th year ({{jalink|1337|Q6039}}), the shrine was reconstructed by {{jalink|Isshiki Noriyuki}} on the orders of {{jalink|Takauji Ashikaga|Q297107}}, but {{jal ink|Tensho|Q1145058}}It is said that it was burnt down by military fire during the {{jalink|Odawara Conquest|Q2622890}} in 18 years ({{jalink|1590|Q6764}}) {{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}.In {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, {{jalink|Keian|Q1146135}} 2nd year ({{jalink|1649|Q6945}}), {{jalink|Tokugawa Iemitsu|Q314481}} donated 7 stones of {{jalink|shuinchi}}. This red stamp letter says ``Tsukuyomisha,'' {{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}, but in "Chogu Engi" this company name is written incorrectly. However, this company name continues to be used in subsequent shuinjo. {{jalink|Betto-ji|Q11396755}} is Gassan-dera Temple, and {{jalink|Gyokuzo-in (Saitama City)|Q18336826|Gyokuzo-in}} was also attached to it. A twenty-three night hall was built in Gassan-ji Temple, and the {{jalink|Seishi Bosatsu|Q1465683}} of {{jalink|Getten|Q1061795|Tsukitenshi}} was enshrined as {{jalink|principal image|Q11520160}} {{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}.It is also listed in "{{jalink|Edo Meisho Zue|Q2704637}}," and many writers, writers, and artists also visit it, perhaps knowing about the god of Tsukimachi and Tsukuyomi. Among them, {{jalink|Kansei|Q1195131}} 3rd year ({{jalink|1791|Q7784}}) {{jalink|April 11th (Lunar Calendar)|Q838941|April 11th}}, the haiku poet {{jalink|Issa Kobayashi|Q312709}} wrote in his ``Kiko'' (Volume 5 of Issa's Complete Works) that he put his forehead to the ``Tsukiyomi no Miya'' located in the deep forest. In addition, {{jalink|Tsuda Daijo}}, a monk who loved traveling, wrote ``20 The shrine, which is called Sanya no Miya (omitted), has been constructed with great care and beauty, and the interior is decorated with so much heart and soul that it is filled with offerings that will astound your eyes if you wander into the middle of nowhere.'' {{Sfn|Saitama Shrines (Saitama Prefectural Shrine Agency)|1998}} Also, {{jalink|Culture (era name)|Q1373146|Bunka}}4 years ({{jalink|1807|Q6909}}) to {{jalink|Tenpo|Q1369045}}5 years ({{jalink|1834|Q759 1}}), the Tokugawa {{jalink|Shimizu family}} traveled to the suburbs of {{jalink|Edo|Q215646}} and visited {{jalink|Hiroshiki Yonin|Q11485425}}'s {{jalink|village}. In his records, Yasushi Omasa}} (whose name was Karyo) said, ``It is said to be a forest of {{jalink|Seishi Bosatsu|Q1465683}}.The head office and the hall of worship all have copper-roofed roofs, and the entire building is {{jalink|vermilion|Q737438| The structure of the shrine, which is painted vermilion and surrounded by azure (omitted), is not Buddhist, so it is likely that in ancient times it was worshiped as a god, but later it was worshiped as a Buddha.''Karyo Murao writes about the situation at that time. Author: Translated by Takatsugu Abe, “Edo Suburbs Roadside Signs: Modern Language Translation” {{jalink|Kodansha Academic Library|Q11632220}}, 2013. .In the 10th year of Tenpo ({{jalink|1839|Q7616}}), {{jalink|Atsutane Hirata|Q2572749}}, a {{jalink|Kokugaku|Q855159}} scholar, visited our company and wrote about the ``Chojinsha.'' In that book, we wrote that the shrine developed from a warehouse to store {{jalink|firstfruits|Q11396544}} to be delivered to {{jalink|Ise Jingu|Q687168}}. After {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}), {{jalink|Modern company ranking system|Q In 712534}}, it was ranked as {{jalink|gosha|Q134917316}}, and in 1898|Q7848}} it was promoted to {{jalink|kensha}}. === Precincts === The shrine building is {{jalink|Gongen-zukuri|Q3155268}}, with the main shrine and worship hall integrated, and was constructed in {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}}5 ({{jalink|1858|Q7684}}) {{Sfn|Cho Shrine (Heibonsha)|2004}}. The old main hall that was used until then is still used as the main hall of Inari Shrine within the shrine grounds. In addition, the shrine grounds within the shrine grounds form a forest of old Zelkova and Mukunoki trees, and have been designated as a natural monument by Saitama City as the ``Cho Shrine Precincts Forest.'' File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) kagura-den.JPG|Kaguraden File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) shin-ike.JPG|Kamiike no Usagi File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) chozusha.JPG|Rabbit of Chozuya File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) komausagi.JPG|Komausagi === Setsusha === [[File:Cho Shrine Old Main Hall (Inari Shrine).jpg|thumb|{{center|Inari Shrine}}{{small|The shrine building is the former main hall of Cho Shrine. Repairs were completed in 2017. The roof was changed from copper sheet to persimmon. }}
{{small|A cultural property designated by Saitama City. }}]] * Inari Shrine *: The shrine building is the former main hall of Chojinja Shrine. The roof is {{jalink|shingled||persimmon shingled}}. According to the ridge tag, it was built in {{jalink|Kyoho|Q1145048}}18 ({{jalink|1733|Q6972}}) and was used as the main shrine until the {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}} years ({{jalink|1854|Q7670}}-{{jalink|1860|Q7691}}). There are many carvings of rabbits, reflecting the Tsukimachi belief of Cho Shrine. This shrine building is designated as a cultural property by Saitama City . * Chogu Tenjin Shrine * Konpira Shrine Several other companies are located within the precincts. File:Tsuki-jinja (Saitama) keidaisha.JPG|{{small|Chogu Tenjin Shrine (front) and Konpira Shrine (back)}} [[File:Sushoson.jpg|Alternative sentence=Sushoson|Thumbnail|{{center|Sushoson Stone Statue}}]] === Festival === List of major festivals held annually at Cho Shrine [http://www.stib.jp/info/data/tsuki.html Cho Shrine] (Saitama Tourism and International Exchange Association, ``Saitama City Tourism and International Exchange Information''). . * Saitan Festival ({{jalink|January 1st|Q2150}}) * Setsubun Festival ({{jalink|February 3rd|Q2315}}) * Spring Festival ({{jalink|April 3rd|Q2509}}) * Great purification ceremony ({{jalink|June 30th|Q2657}}) * Shinko Festival ({{jalink|July 19th|Q2726}}) * '''Reitaisai'''' ({{jalink|July 20th|Q2728}}) - Follows the historic Hohei Day on June 20th of the lunar calendar, which is the origin of the Reitaisai. * Autumn Festival ({{jalink|November 23rd|Q3021}}) * Otoshi City Festival ({{jalink|Twelfth Day|Q22123263}}) ({{jalink|December 12th|Q2369}}) === Cultural assets === === Cultural property designated by Saitama City === * Tangible cultural properties{{PDFlink|[https://www.city.saitama.lg.jp/004/005/006/003/p001267_d/fil/bunkazaiitiran.pdf "List of designated and registered cultural properties in Saitama City"]}} (Saitama City homepage). ** Old main hall (with wooden tag {{jalink|Kyoho|Q1145048}} with an inscription of 18 years) (building) **: Edo period. {{jalink|Showa|Q327567}}53 ({{jalink|1978|Q2483}}) {{jalink|March 29|Q2459}} Designated . ** Thirty-six immortal paintings on a fan, 18 (paintings) **: Edo period. {{jalink|Thirty-six poets|Q671039|Thirty-six poets and paintings}} written in ink and paper on paper, of which 18 still exist. Dedicated in {{jalink|Kanbun|Q1146228}}9 ({{jalink|1669|Q7602}}). Designated on March 29, 1978 . ** Mikoshi phoenix (craft) **: Muromachi period. {{jalink|Phoenix|Q527525}} was attached to the top of {{jalink|Mikoshi|Q979940}}. Designated on March 29, 1978 . ** Cho Shrine plaque (calligraphy)**: Edo period. Zelkova wood {{jalink|flat plaque|Q5358873}}. The words ``Cho Shrine'' are written in block letters on the front. Written by {{jalink|Sadanobu Matsudaira|Q284038}} in 2nd year ({{jalink|1802|Q6883}}). Designated on March 29, 1978 . ** Chogu Engi (ancient document) **: Edo period. Selected by Jakudo the 12th {{jalink|Gyokuzoin (Saitama City)|Q18336826|Gyokuzoin}} in the 8th year of Kanbun ({{jalink|1668|Q7595}}). Designated on March 29, 1978 . * Natural monument ** Forest within the precincts of Cho Shrine **: Showa 45 ({{jalink|1970|Q2474}}) Designated {{jalink|March 10|Q2397}}. === Seven Wonders === At Cho Shrine, the following seven wonders are handed down. # No torii gate #: It is said that on the orders of {{jalink|Wahime no Mikoto|Q4388732}}, the shrine gates and torii gates, which were obstacles to the transportation of preparations, were removed. # No pine trees #: It is said that this name originates from the fact that there was an older sister god and a younger brother god in this area, but eventually the younger god went to Omiya and did not come back even though the older sister was waiting for him, so the older sister said that she did not want to wait any longer. It is also said that he was poked in the eye by a pine tree in the shrine grounds while his sister deity was waiting for him. # If you release a fish into Mitarai Pond (also known as Gourd Pond, which is now extinct), the fish will have one eye. # The rabbit is {{jalink|divine messenger|Q10444973}} # {{jalink|Nichiren|Q311478|Nichiren Saint}}Zelkova connecting pieces #: It is said that Nichiren, who was exiled to {{jalink|Sado Island|Q998623}}, tied a piece to a zelkova tree to pray for a safe birth for a woman there who was suffering from difficult childbirth. # No flies # No mosquitoes === Local information === ; Location * {{jalink|Saitama Prefecture|Q128186}}{{jalink|Saitama City|Q134635}}{{jalink|Urawa Ward|Q1154583}}{{jalink|Kishimachi (Saitama City)|Q11476004|Kishimachi}}3-17-25 ; Transportation access * Railway: {{jalink|East Japan Railway|Q499071}} (JR East)
